EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm watch was issued at 11:58 PM that covered most of northeast Nebraska. A cold front moving across Nebraska initiated storms in central Nebraska that moved through eastern Nebraska and western Iowa producing widespread damage with the highest reported winds at 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter estimated wind speeds at 71 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushing across central South Dakota and Nebraska ignited severe storms overnight. With elevated instability of 1000-1500 J/kg and an increasing low-level jet, numerous storms formed along the 700 hPa baroclinic zone with a few taking on bowing characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were strewn across Highway 59. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through toward northern Iowa by mid-afternoon, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary. With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated to around 60 mph accompanied ping-pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across northeast Iowa during the early morning hours of September 6th. These storms dropped some hail with most of it being less than an inch in diameter. However, one storm did produce quarter sized hail in Oelwein (Fayette County). A mesonet station in Saratoga (Howard County) measured a 59 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Saratoga measured a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days and nights of severe storms that impacted the Midwest occurred between June 3-5.  On June 4th, a line of severe storms moved southeast, through eastern Iowa into Illinois. Damaging winds and heavy rain occurred with this line of storms, which produced some flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place as several weak disturbances moved across the area through the day.  This led to the development of thunderstorms across southeast Iowa, some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a heavy wooden outdoor dog crate was blown across the yard.  They estimated the winds to be 60 to 65 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass was in place as several weak disturbances moved across the area through the day.  This led to the development of thunderstorms across southeast Iowa, some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that 4 to 6 inch tree limbs were down and the power was out.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown down at Arlington Ave and East 15th Street in Davenport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days and nights of severe storms that impacted the Midwest occurred between June 3-5.  On June 4th, a line of severe storms moved southeast, through eastern Iowa into Illinois. Damaging winds and heavy rain occurred with this line of storms, which produced some flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Davenport, Iowa Airport ASOS reported a 61 MPH gust. Winds were sustained at 45 mph for over 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the area during the late morning and afternoon interacted with a nearly stationary boundary across the area and produced thunderstorms along the Highway 30 corridor.  These storms produced hail up to the size of quarters as well as gusty winds that brought down limbs and downed a tree across Highway 30 in southwest Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of damage to corn crops.  The corn was laying down in spots with some leaves stripped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the area during the late morning and afternoon interacted with a nearly stationary boundary across the area and produced thunderstorms along the Highway 30 corridor.  These storms produced hail up to the size of quarters as well as gusty winds that brought down limbs and downed a tree across Highway 30 in southwest Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iowa DOT reported that a large tree was down blocking both lanes of Highway 30 east of Wheatland near were the highway crosses the Wapsipinicon River. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a large tree branch down, reported via social media. Time estimated via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the morning of June 9 remnants of Tropical Storm Cristobal pushed north into Iowa, drenching central and eastern Iowa for most of the day before lifting into the Great Lakes region by evening. The quick exit of Cristobal was due in part to a strong shortwave moving across the northern plains that helped to kick Cristobal from the area. This secondary system moved into Iowa the evening of June 9th, and while for the most part remained sub-severe, damaging winds were reported in west-central Iowa with a stronger cell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported winds of 60 to 70 MPH in Griswold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low to the northwest of Iowa allowed for convection to develop in the warm sector and along the surface boundary as it pushed across northern Iowa through the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These storms caused damaging winds and large hail across northern and central Iowa. Localized areas received heavy rain that resulted in flooding overnight. Another shortwave embedded within the parent longwave trough allowed for additional rain across the state on the 22nd that lasted through the afternoon. This second round of rain exacerbated the ongoing flooded areas, resulting in flooding that lingered into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported multiple tree limbs and power lines down in Iowa Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary remained fairly stationary across the state with environmental parameters continuing to support storm development. Storms across central Iowa produced some wind damage, downing trees and snapping a power pole. A tornado also occurred, cutting through Johnston, a northern Des Moines suburb. Tornado damage was largely confined to trees and powerlines with no structural damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down and power poles snapped between Newton and Kellogg. Reports relayed by WOI, WHO, and KCCI. Time estimated via radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured by Newton AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds removed a few tree limbs up to 4 to 5 inches in diameter. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave embedded in northwest flow moved across Iowa, providing a focus from storm development in an environment that already had MLCAPE values of 2500-3000 J/kg and effective bulk shear of around 40 knots. Convection that occurred earlier in the morning of July 11th also provided a focus for storm development on lingering outflow boundaries. This allowed for swaths of large hail across the state, with reports of up to tennis ball sized hail in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media picture showed damage to sweet corn east of Coalville. Possible that damage was due to a mix of hail and wind, which flattened the corn. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms associated with a cold front moved through eastern Nebraska into southwest Iowa. It produced large hail early on and strong winds late in the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iowa DOT site measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across portions of northeast Iowa during the morning of July 11th. These storms dropped quarter sized hail across portions of Chickasaw and Fayette Counties. The hail damaged some crops east of Lawler (Chickasaw County). The storms also produced some damaging winds that blew down trees west of Randalia (Fayette County). Some farm out buildings were blown down and corn flattened northeast of Westgate (Fayette County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several farm out buildings were damaged or blown down and some corn was flattened northeast of Westgate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across much of northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 9th. Damaging winds from these storms blew down trees in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), Oelwein (Fayette County) and blew a roof of a building in West Union (Fayette County). Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Waukon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across much of northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 9th. Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Oelwein. Some large tree branches landed on houses but only caused minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust to 60 mph measured by hand-held anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of July 7th, severe storms moved across northeast Iowa, bringing damaging winds and heavy rain. Tree branch and corn damage were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb was broken off outside of the TV news station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of July 7th, severe storms moved across northeast Iowa, bringing damaging winds and heavy rain. Tree branch and corn damage were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A young healthy tree was split in half on Byson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of severe thunderstorms produced a derecho which tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Monday, August 10th, resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. The cost of damage from this storm was extreme, reaching an estimated 7.5 billion dollars along the path of these storms from Iowa through Illinois.  Two brief tornadoes have been confirmed within the widespread swath of wind damage, one just north of the Eastern Iowa Airport in Linn County Iowa, and one just south of Freeport, Illinois. These tornadoes have been designated as EF-U, as there was no observable damage directly attributable to the tornadoes from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. While an average of two derechos a year occur across the area, a derecho of this intensity is a roughly once-in-a-decade occurrence. Another unusual aspect of this derecho was the duration of the strong winds, with gusts over 60 mph for over an hour at some locations.||The most extreme winds, estimated at 110-140 mph, destroyed or damaged numerous outbuildings, barns, grain bins, homes, mobile homes, apartment buildings, trees, and power poles in parts of Benton, Linn, Jones, Cedar, and Clinton Counties. The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured wind gust from the south side of the derecho event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread straight-line winds that produced extensive damage were reported throughout Jones County, associated with a derecho. These winds lasted around an hour in total at any one location, even though the initial line of storms moved out quickly. Damaging straight-line winds continued and were associated with the rear inflow jet. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be 80 to 100 MPH for much of the county, with an area of 100  MPH or higher in the south central part of the county. These estimates were determined based off damage reports and photos submitted through social media. The duration of strong winds caused extensive damage to most trees, powerlines, crops, and structures in their path. The widespread damage also resulted in long duration power outages.

Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 inch tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video sent through social media showing a tree falling in a yard and over a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous round of showers and thunderstorms moved across eastern Iowa during the day on the 22nd as a storm system interacted with numerous outflow boundaries and a quasi-stationary front across the area. These storms brought heavy rains of 1 to 2 inches in Benton and Buchanan Counties that caused flooding, as well as severe wind gusts in southeast Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a tree with several large branches off. along with corn stocks blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Department reported a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low to the northwest of Iowa allowed for convection to develop in the warm sector and along the surface boundary as it pushed across northern Iowa through the afternoon and evening of the 21st. These storms caused damaging winds and large hail across northern and central Iowa. Localized areas received heavy rain that resulted in flooding overnight. Another shortwave embedded within the parent longwave trough allowed for additional rain across the state on the 22nd that lasted through the afternoon. This second round of rain exacerbated the ongoing flooded areas, resulting in flooding that lingered into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 60 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee estimated winds of at least 60 mph between 4:36am to 4:39am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moved across the midwest, pulling a surface boundary across the state. In the afternoon weak convection developed along this boundary, but most storms tended to be short-lived in nature with relatively weak shear profiles. These storms resulted in mainly wind damage reports across east-central Iowa. Convection continued to bubble through the evening with continued wind damage reports. Shortly after midnight convection out of the Dakotas made it to northern Iowa. This broken line of storms pushed across the state all night, with essentially no breaks in reports of large hail and damaging winds through sunrise. Additionally, some locations received heavy rain. Storms cleared the Des Moines metro area shortly after five in the morning and diminished in strength shortly after that.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight convection across the central plains moved into Iowa during the early morning hours of June 28th. While storms had diminished in strength, a damaging wind gust did occur. The precipitation associated with this mesoscale convective vortex continued to weaken through the morning, however, the environment in its wake exhibited high low-level vorticity that supported funnel cloud development later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager relayed a report of a 50 foot pine tree knocked over. Event was described as a burst of wind with other objects blown across the yard. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from southwest Iowa to north central Iowa Saturday afternoon. Storms developed, moved across a warm front, and four tornadoes touched down over eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois.  A few other storms brought small hail and damaging wind to portions of the region Saturday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management relayed a report of measured high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Six tornadoes occurred across central Iowa during the afternoon.  The tornadoes remained in rural areas with some damage to farm buildings and trees.  No injuries or deaths were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of destroyed machine shed. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and remnants of a nocturnal mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) slowly moved across Iowa in the late morning to early afternoon hours. With moderate CAPE in the 1500-3000 J/kg range and shear of 20-30 kts, conditions were sufficient to support severe storm development. Storms produced winds gusting up to 70 mph at times, leaving behind significant tree damage in a few locations across north central and east central Iowa, along with periods of heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of a large tree crushing a pickup truck. Time estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large longwave trough was in place across much of the western and central US with an embedded shortwave. This provided a focus for storm development as it moved into a humid and unstable environment. Scattered convection eventually increased in coverage through the evening, producing quarter sized hail and damaging wind gusts. As convection grew upscale and moved into southern Iowa the severe threat diminished, though heavy rain continued to fall through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported damage to several large trees.

Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a building in West Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front across Kansas and Missouri lifted north into Iowa the evening of September 5th, fueled by the increasing low-level jet. Shortly after midnight on the 6th, the front was situated over northern Iowa and convection out of Minnesota moved southeast into Iowa along the front and into a favorable convective environment as MUCAPE values rose between 2000 to 3000 J/kg. Steep mid level lapse rates of 8 C/km and deep layer shear of 50+ knots allowed for convection to quickly reach severe criteria. Golf ball sized hail and significant wind damage occured with these overnight storms. They were also efficient rain producers, with locations reporting between two to over 4 inches of rain in the heaviest cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clarion airport AWOS reported a 51 knot gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of severe thunderstorms produced a derecho which tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Monday, August 10th, resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. The cost of damage from this storm was extreme, reaching an estimated 7.5 billion dollars along the path of these storms from Iowa through Illinois.  Two brief tornadoes have been confirmed within the widespread swath of wind damage, one just north of the Eastern Iowa Airport in Linn County Iowa, and one just south of Freeport, Illinois. These tornadoes have been designated as EF-U, as there was no observable damage directly attributable to the tornadoes from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. While an average of two derechos a year occur across the area, a derecho of this intensity is a roughly once-in-a-decade occurrence. Another unusual aspect of this derecho was the duration of the strong winds, with gusts over 60 mph for over an hour at some locations.||The most extreme winds, estimated at 110-140 mph, destroyed or damaged numerous outbuildings, barns, grain bins, homes, mobile homes, apartment buildings, trees, and power poles in parts of Benton, Linn, Jones, Cedar, and Clinton Counties. The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread straight-line winds that produced extensive damage were reported throughout Benton County, associated with a derecho. These winds lasted around an hour in total, even though the initial line of convection moved out fast. Damaging straight-line winds continued and were associated with the rear inflow jet. Maximum wind speeds were reported up to 80 mph for much of the county, with areas in central Benton County that had wind speed estimates from 120 mph wind or greater. The maximum measured unofficial wind gust was 126 mph in Atkins, IA. These estimates were determined based off damage surveys and photos submitted through social media. These strong winds extensively damaged most trees, powerlines, crops, and many structures in their path. This resulted in long duration power outages.

A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread straight-line winds that produced extensive damage were reported throughout Washington County, associated with a derecho. These winds lasted around an hour in total at any one location, even though the initial line of storms moved out quickly. Damaging straight-line winds continued and were associated with the rear inflow jet. Maximum wind speeds were estimated to be 60 to 80 MPH. These estimates were determined based off damage reports and photos submitted through social media. The duration of strong winds caused extensive damage to some trees, powerlines, crops, and a few structures in their path. The widespread damage also resulted in long duration power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of severe thunderstorms produced a derecho which tracked across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois on the afternoon of Monday, August 10th, resulting in widespread straight line wind damage. The cost of damage from this storm was extreme, reaching an estimated 7.5 billion dollars along the path of these storms from Iowa through Illinois.  Two brief tornadoes have been confirmed within the widespread swath of wind damage, one just north of the Eastern Iowa Airport in Linn County Iowa, and one just south of Freeport, Illinois. These tornadoes have been designated as EF-U, as there was no observable damage directly attributable to the tornadoes from which an EF-scale rating could be assigned. While an average of two derechos a year occur across the area, a derecho of this intensity is a roughly once-in-a-decade occurrence. Another unusual aspect of this derecho was the duration of the strong winds, with gusts over 60 mph for over an hour at some locations.||The most extreme winds, estimated at 110-140 mph, destroyed or damaged numerous outbuildings, barns, grain bins, homes, mobile homes, apartment buildings, trees, and power poles in parts of Benton, Linn, Jones, Cedar, and Clinton Counties. The Cedar Rapids area was particularly hard hit. One fatality occurred in Linn County, as a tree fell on a cyclist. Several homes, apartment complexes, and businesses sustained damage consistent with 130-140 mph winds. Radio transmission towers in Marion and Clinton, Iowa collapsed due to winds estimated around 130 mph. Wind gusts of 80-100 mph were common as the line of storms moved through the Quad Cities area and then through northwest Illinois. A small pocket of winds estimated at 100-110 mph impacted Princeton, IL in Bureau County, where a 150 foot communications tower collapsed and numerous power poles were snapped. In addition to the damage, numerous long-duration power outages occurred across the region. Damage to crops was considerable along the derecho path. Numerous semi trucks were also blown off roadways along the path of the derecho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A healthy tree was split by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days and nights of severe storms that impacted the Midwest occurred between June 3-5.  On June 4th, a line of severe storms moved southeast, through eastern Iowa into Illinois. Damaging winds and heavy rain occurred with this line of storms, which produced some flash flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of showers and thunderstorms affected the area June 3-5.  An isolated storm produced sub-severe winds in Buchanan County, and then the storm became severe as it moved through Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported that part of a roof was torn off of rural building.  The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave embedded in northwest flow moved across Iowa, providing a focus from storm development in an environment that already had MLCAPE values of 2500-3000 J/kg and effective bulk shear of around 40 knots. Convection that occurred earlier in the morning of July 11th also provided a focus for storm development on lingering outflow boundaries. This allowed for swaths of large hail across the state, with reports of up to tennis ball sized hail in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mason City airport reported a wind gust of 54 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional severe thunderstorms developed near a slowly advancing cold front during the late afternoon and evening hours across southeast South Dakota and northwest Iowa. MLCAPE reached a bit over 1000 J/kg with marginal deep-layer shear at around 30 knots. A strong wake low developed within the stratiform region behind the main line of convection, and produced high winds and some damage in parts of northwest Iowa later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station SPW 3 miles west northwest of Spencer measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of July 8th, strong to severe storms impacted Davenport, Iowa with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at the corner of 35th street and Marquette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of July 8th, strong to severe storms impacted Davenport, Iowa with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down by thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station SLB near Storm Lake measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across much of northeast Iowa during the late morning and early afternoon of July 9th. Damaging winds from these storms blew down trees in St. Ansgar (Mitchell County), Oelwein (Fayette County) and blew a roof of a building in West Union (Fayette County). Trees and a power lines were blown down in Postville  and Waukon (Allamakee County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches were blown down in New Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A three inch diameter tree branch blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a boundary from southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, producing damaging wind and spotty large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were taken down by the strong winds. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds flattened crops 3 to 4 miles east of Milford. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread corn flattened due to thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois Saturday, July 11, producing widespread wind damage and very large hail. The first line of storms came through during the morning, producing damaging wind gusts and reports of quarter to ping pong ball size hail from Independence to Tipton Iowa. A wind gust of 65 MPH was measured just west of Independence. The second round of thunderstorms developed as a result of an upper level disturbance interacting with an atmosphere characterized by extremely high instability with modest vertical wind shear. Activity initially formed in north central Iowa, producing golf ball to two inch hail from Fort Dodge to Waterloo Iowa. Eventually, storms made their way towards the Cedar Rapids/Iowa City metro areas, producing widespread wind damage and hail up to baseball size. These storms continued through the Quad Cities metro east towards Princeton, Sterling, and Hennepin Illinois, producing additional reports of wind damage and winds of 70 to 90 mph. Numerous power outages were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of intense thunderstorms moved across southeast Iowa and western Illinois in the morning hours of July 19th. This cluster of storms brought very heavy rain, and very strong damaging winds. Several locations saw winds over 75 mph, and had significant property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight inch diameter tree branch was blown down at Lefflee and Wheeler St. intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs two to four inches in diameter were snapped. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms that developed in the early afternoon took advantage of the very high instability and had no trouble becoming strong, and even severe, in a short amount of time. Storms initially formed along and north of the Highway 30 corridor in eastern Iowa, produced widespread damage to crops in Buchanan and Delaware counties, and eventually went on to produce a wind gust near 60 mph at the Dubuque Regional Airport. Storms across this area eventually merged and slowed down, leading to localized rainfall amounts of 4-5 inches. This very heavy rainfall produced flash flooding in the Dubuque metro, with law enforcement reporting several road closures along Highway 20.|Storms continued to form along a line to the south through the mid afternoon, and reached the Mississippi River around 6-7 PM. Several reports of wind damage to trees were received from McDonough county to the Quad Cities metro area. A few reports of funnel clouds were also received from the Kirkwood, IL area, but no tornado was ever observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported by the Dubuque Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tracked across central Iowa into eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and northwest Illinois late Friday afternoon and into the overnight hours. These storms brought heavy rain, frequent lightning, damaging winds, and large hail to the region.  The hardest hit areas were Keokuk county in Iowa, towards Henry county in Iowa, where 50-70 mph winds occurred.  A weak EF-1 tornado occurred near Atkinson, IL in Henry county in Illinois after 1015 pm and caused damage to trees and outbuildings at two farmsteads. ||Widespread rainfall amounts over 1 inch generally fell south of a line from Sigourney and Maquoketa, IA to Freeport, IL.  This rainfall combined with the previous days rain brought rain totals of 2-4 inches to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines reported blown down. A machine shed was damaged and blown onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Basic Summary: A cluster of storms in northeast Nebraska and southeast South Dakota developed into the destructive derecho that raced eastward across large portions of Iowa, northern Illinois, southern Wisconsin, northern Indiana, and southern Michigan during the morning and afternoon of August 10th. The strongest winds were experienced in central and east-central Iowa where 100+ mph winds were observed. Some of the strongest winds were in an area from roughly Marshalltown, IA eastward along the Highway 30 corridor to the Mississippi River, where estimated winds exceeded 120+ mph and approached 140 mph. A number of QLCS tornadoes were also experienced within the Highway 30 corridor. One of the most remarkable aspects of the derecho was the duration of severe winds, with many areas experiencing at least periodic, if not persistent, severe level (58+ mph) winds over a roughly 30 minute time frame. As the derecho moved eastward out of Iowa, it continued to expand, but weakened from its peak strength. Winds continued to exceed severe criteria (58+ mph) in Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, and Michigan and began to produce additional QLCS tornadoes across northern Illinois before fading out across northern Indiana and southern Michigan.||In the wake of the derecho, damage was substantial in many areas, rural and urban. Many agriculture fields, mainly corn, were flattened within the swaths of strongest winds. Initial estimates suggest at least 850,000 acres of partial or complete crop loss occurred from this event. Within urban and wooded areas extensive tree and structure damage was seen, including complete roof failure in a few instances. At its peak, total estimated customers without power exceeded 1.4 million across the affected states. In total, early estimates on losses (agriculture, infrastructure, private property) have exceeded $7.5 billion across the states affected. ||Environment: Broadly speaking, it was a ripe day for thunderstorms across the affected area with MUCAPE values often exceeding 3500-4000 J/kg by the mid morning hours concurrent with mid-upper level short wave support, and indications that any cold pool development would be strong (difference between cold pool and ambient air) and potentially long lived. For detailed environmental analysis, including soundings, please view the various local NWS office event review pages: NWS Des Moines, NWS Quad Cities, NWS Chicago, NWS Milwaukee, NWS Central Illinois, NWS Northern Indiana, or NWS Indianapolis.||Additional Details: Numerous injuries occurred during the derecho and in the aftermath during cleanup, including 4 direct and indirect deaths of which 3 occurred in Iowa (two in Poweshiek County and one in Linn County) and 1 in Indiana (Fort Wayne).||Disclaimer: The August 10, 2020 derecho resulted in widespread high-end damage across large portions of central and southern Iowa. Given the size and scope of the event and understanding that final damage and loss estimates (agriculture, infrastructure, and private property) will take months to complete, the NWS is not able to estimate the monetary impact at this time. Values may be added as more data becomes from various sources in future months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree limb 6 inches in diameter and 20 feet long was downed. Several smaller limbs were downed as well.

The strongest winds were experienced in central and east-central Iowa where 100+ mph winds were observed. Some of the strongest winds were in an area from roughly Marshalltown, IA eastward along the Highway 30 corridor to the Mississippi River, where estimated winds exceeded 120+ mph and approached 140 mph. A number of QLCS tornadoes were also experienced within the Highway 30 corridor. One of the most remarkable aspects of the derecho was the duration of severe winds, with many areas experiencing at least periodic, if not persistent, severe level (58+ mph) winds over a roughly 30 minute time frame. As the derecho moved eastward out of Iowa, it continued to expand, but weakened from its peak strength. Winds continued to exceed severe criteria (58+ mph) in Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, and Michigan and began to produce additional QLCS tornadoes across northern Illinois before fading out across northern Indiana and southern Michigan.||In the wake of the derecho, damage was substantial in many areas, rural and urban. Many agriculture fields, mainly corn, were flattened within the swaths of strongest winds. Initial estimates suggest at least 850,000 acres of partial or complete crop loss occurred from this event. Within urban and wooded areas extensive tree and structure damage was seen, including complete roof failure in a few instances. At its peak, total estimated customers without power exceeded 1.4 million across the affected states. In total, early estimates on losses (agriculture, infrastructure, private property) have exceeded $7.5 billion across the states affected. ||Environment: Broadly speaking, it was a ripe day for thunderstorms across the affected area with MUCAPE values often exceeding 3500-4000 J/kg by the mid morning hours concurrent with mid-upper level short wave support, and indications that any cold pool development would be strong (difference between cold pool and ambient air) and potentially long lived. For detailed environmental analysis, including soundings, please view the various local NWS office event review pages: NWS Des Moines, NWS Quad Cities, NWS Chicago, NWS Milwaukee, NWS Central Illinois, NWS Northern Indiana, or NWS Indianapolis.||Additional Details: Numerous injuries occurred during the derecho and in the aftermath during cleanup, including 4 direct and indirect deaths of which 3 occurred in Iowa (two in Poweshiek County and one in Linn County) and 1 in Indiana (Fort Wayne).||Disclaimer: The August 10, 2020 derecho resulted in widespread high-end damage across large portions of central and southern Iowa. Given the size and scope of the event and understanding that final damage and loss estimates (agriculture, infrastructure, and private property) will take months to complete, the NWS is not able to estimate the monetary impact at this time. Values may be added as more data becomes from various sources in future months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 8 inch tree limbs down, power lines down, and trees down all over town.

A number of QLCS tornadoes were also experienced within the Highway 30 corridor. One of the most remarkable aspects of the derecho was the duration of severe winds, with many areas experiencing at least periodic, if not persistent, severe level (58+ mph) winds over a roughly 30 minute time frame. As the derecho moved eastward out of Iowa, it continued to expand, but weakened from its peak strength. Winds continued to exceed severe criteria (58+ mph) in Illinois, Wisconsin, Indiana, and Michigan and began to produce additional QLCS tornadoes across northern Illinois before fading out across northern Indiana and southern Michigan.||In the wake of the derecho, damage was substantial in many areas, rural and urban. Many agriculture fields, mainly corn, were flattened within the swaths of strongest winds. Initial estimates suggest at least 850,000 acres of partial or complete crop loss occurred from this event. Within urban and wooded areas extensive tree and structure damage was seen, including complete roof failure in a few instances. At its peak, total estimated customers without power exceeded 1.4 million across the affected states. In total, early estimates on losses (agriculture, infrastructure, private property) have exceeded $7.5 billion across the states affected. ||Environment: Broadly speaking, it was a ripe day for thunderstorms across the affected area with MUCAPE values often exceeding 3500-4000 J/kg by the mid morning hours concurrent with mid-upper level short wave support, and indications that any cold pool development would be strong (difference between cold pool and ambient air) and potentially long lived. For detailed environmental analysis, including soundings, please view the various local NWS office event review pages: NWS Des Moines, NWS Quad Cities, NWS Chicago, NWS Milwaukee, NWS Central Illinois, NWS Northern Indiana, or NWS Indianapolis.||Additional Details: Numerous injuries occurred during the derecho and in the aftermath during cleanup, including 4 direct and indirect deaths of which 3 occurred in Iowa (two in Poweshiek County and one in Linn County) and 1 in Indiana (Fort Wayne).||Disclaimer: The August 10, 2020 derecho resulted in widespread high-end damage across large portions of central and southern Iowa. Given the size and scope of the event and understanding that final damage and loss estimates (agriculture, infrastructure, and private property) will take months to complete, the NWS is not able to estimate the monetary impact at this time. Values may be added as more data becomes from various sources in future months.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported trees down. Wind was still blowing at time of report.
